summary: DeVault excoriates Plaid for asking users to enter their bank credentials into third-party forms, arguing this fundamentally undermines decades of security education about verifying domains before entering passwords. He acknowledges the legitimate problem Plaid addresses — banks' slow adoption of modern integration technology — but argues that tearing down online security pillars for mild convenience is indefensible. He notes Plaid's $58M lawsuit payout for scraping and selling bank data, placing it in the 'move fast and break laws' category alongside Uber and AirBnB. He calls for aggressive regulation and holds both Plaid's B2B customers and employees morally complicit.
key_points: Plaid collects bank credentials on domains operated by neither the bank nor Plaid, undermining the most fundamental piece of online security advice
The problem Plaid solves is real — bank account verification via micro-deposits is slow and annoying — but the solution destroys security norms for marginal convenience
Plaid's $58M lawsuit settlement and potential CFAA violations place it in the 'move fast and break laws' startup category
B2B customers cannot offload ethical due diligence onto vendors — adopting Plaid makes them complicit
Banks are modernizing globally, making Plaid's security-destroying shortcut increasingly unnecessary
money_quotes: Plaid wants to throw out all of those years of hard work and ask users to enter their freaking bank credentials into a third-party form.
The third-party service provider in question is the end-user's bank.
Tearing down the pillars of online security in exchange for a mild convenience is ridiculous.
This platform can only exist if they are either willfully malignant or grossly incompetent.
Plaid thus joins the ranks of Uber, AirBnB, and others like them in my reckoning as a 'move fast and break laws' company.
They've built something that they know is wrong, and are hoping that they can outrun the regulators.
Shame on their B2B customers as well, who cannot, such as they may like to, offload ethical due-diligence onto their vendors.
key_insight: Plaid's business model fundamentally contradicts decades of security education by training users to enter bank credentials into third-party forms, and no amount of convenience justifies normalizing this anti-pattern.
